---
author:
  - name: Martin Ho
    email: martinkh.ho@mail.utoronto.ca
date: today
date-format: long
---


# Welcome {.landing-title}

## Setup required

1. Git ![Git logo](assets/images/git_logo.png){.inline-logo height=1em}
2. GitHub ![GitHub logo](assets/images/github_logo.png){.inline-logo height=1em}
3. VS Code ![VS Code logo](assets/images/vscode_logo.png){.inline-logo height=1em}


## Motivation

In school, we learn many methods for performing statistical analyses. But science is more than the analyses: it is also about:

+----------------------------------------+----------------------------------------------------------------------------------+
| Open science concept                   | Focus                                                                            |
+========================================+==================================================================================+
| **FAIR data principles**               | Maximize the reusability of data assets:*                                        |
| [@wilkinson2016fair]                   |                                                                                  |
+----------------------------------------+----------------------------------------------------------------------------------+
| - Findability                          | - Data/metadata are discoverable for both humans and machines                    |
| - Accessibility                        | - Once found, data/metadata are retrievable                                      |
| - Interoperability                     | - Data/metadata have standardized formats so that they can be integrated with    |
|                                        |   other data systems                                                             |
| - Reusability                          | - Data/metadata are richly described and licensed                                |
+----------------------------------------+----------------------------------------------------------------------------------+
| **Transparency**                       | Understand study decisions:                                                      |
+----------------------------------------+----------------------------------------------------------------------------------+
|                                        | - Provenance                                                                     |
|                                        | - Definitions                                                                    |
|                                        | - Methods documentation                                                          |
+----------------------------------------+----------------------------------------------------------------------------------+
| **Reproducibility**                    | Verify study workflow:                                                           |
+----------------------------------------+----------------------------------------------------------------------------------+
|                                        | - Version control                                                                |
|                                        | - Dependency management                                                          |
+----------------------------------------+----------------------------------------------------------------------------------+
\* *FAIR provides the infrastructure that enables transparency and reproducibility.*

Good scientific papers that do not have strong reach may end up collecting dust on the shelf; their code, data, and other materials go to waste. The idea behind FAIR is that research outputs should be shared so that others can discover and use/adapt them for secondary use, validation studies, and meta-analyses [@tai2025fairification]. Note that these principles do not just apply to data, but also to tools and workflows. Even studies that must redact sensitive data (e.g., individual-level public health information) or code that reveals the underlying data models can still achieve a high degree of 'FAIRness' [@wilkinson2016fair].

The field of pharmacoepidemiology has been pushing for transparency and reproducibility, and by association, for FAIR principles. Researchers can track transparency across 5 domains (adapted from the [Transparency and Openness Promotion (TOP) Guidelines](https://www.cos.io/initiatives/top-guidelines), which target journals) [@wang2024building]:

1. Protocol and amendments are available and follow structured templates such as HARPER [@wang2023harmonized]
2. Protocol is pre-registered (i.e., before conducting analyses)
3. Data is available (concerns about privacy may be addressed using synthetic data or protected-access repositories [@tazare2024sharing])
4. Code and supporting material (e.g., algorithms, codelists, data dictionaries, forms) are available (including for analyses, tables, and figures)
5. Manuscript follows the appropriate reporting checklist (e.g., STROBE, RECORD-PE)

And in terms of reproducibility, there are many potential threats to scientific rigour:

![Potential threats to the scientific method (HARKing stands for hypothesizing after the results are known) [@munafo2017manifesto]](assets/images/nature-s415620160021-fig-001.png)

Something as simple as badges [@kidwell2016badges] can encourage data sharing and thus transparency and reproducibility. The International Society for Pharmacoepidemiology (ISPE) now encourages the display of Open Science Badges on publications and conference presentations [@wang2024building; @blohowiak2013badges].

Tazare *et al.* [@tazare2024sharing] reviewed all studies published in the *Pharmacoepidemiology and Drug Safety (PDS)* journal between 2017 and 2022 to see how frequently code was shared. They found that code sharing was low, but increased from 1.8% in 2017 to 9.5% in 2022. 

![Proportion of articles sharing programming code over time [@tazare2024sharing]](assets/images/pds5856-fig-0002-m.jpg){width=600px}

![Proportion of articles following other transparency practices. (A) Data sharing, (B) Codelist sharing (at least one), (C) Preregistration, and (D) Reported adherence to a reporting guideline. [@tazare2024sharing]](assets/images/pds5856-fig-0003-m.jpg){width=600px}

Code sharing has many advantages, including facilitating replication and reuse, detecting errors and misconduct, encouraging documentation, and promoting trust. Nonetheless, code sharing also has barriers. Privacy is a major concern, but another is protecting intellectual property rights. Strategies to address this include licensing, graphical study design diagrams, and pseudocode [@tazare2024sharing]. *Where* authors share code is also important. Most studies that shared code did so as supplementary material [@tazare2024sharing]. However, this is difficult to discover, not indexed in search engines, does not support complex filing structures, and requires copy-pasting [@weberpals2025lets].

![Location of code, when shared [@tazare2024sharing]](assets/images/pds5856-fig-0001-m.png){width=600px}

There are many other aspects of [open science](02_open_science.qmd) [@centerforopensciencewhat]. For this session, we will only focus on the technical aspects of open science that facilitate reproducible analytic workflows [@weberpals2024fairification]. While there are many ways to implement these practices (e.g., GitHub vs. GitLab, Quarto vs. R Markdown, GUI vs. CLI), the tools and workflows presented here represent one opinionated approach chosen for their simplicity and ease of adoption.


## Declaration of generative AI and AI-assisted technologies

All instructional material in this book was written by the author. Generative AI did not determine the content or technical recommendations presented here, though OpenAI ChatGPT (GPT-5.6-Sol, reasoning: instant) helped proofread. OpenAI Codex (GPT-5.6-Terra, reasoning: high) helped format the book. The author reviewed, edited, and verified the suggestions as needed and takes full responsibility for the final product.


::: {.content-visible when-format="html"}
## References
:::
